note: Greek tokens per pure-asemic-low-T vox (n=49), measured directly with each model's tokenizer (no weights). Tests the byte-fragmentation mechanism for the deep-Greek effect.
result: Monotonic: more Greek fragmentation -> stronger deep-Greek name-adjacency. Precision (4-bit Gemma+Mistral at opposite ends) and size (3B/7B/9B) do not explain it. Direct measure, not vocab-size proxy.

nonname_falsifier_mistral.note: Non-name-Greek falsifier on Mistral: UNINFORMATIVE. The two cohorts differ on THREE axes at once -- fragmentation (the non-name cohort fragments ~2x less, 5.15 vs 10.73 Greek tok/str), lexicality (non-name = real Greek; voces = asemic), and namehood -- so the binary 'name-specific' verdict cannot adjudicate, in either direction. NOT a within-model confirmation. The purpose-built fix (fragmentation- AND lexicality-matched arms) is notebooks/voces_falsifier_v2_lexicality.ipynb. See src/check_nonname_fragmentation.py and FINDINGS.md 3b.
nonname_falsifier_mistral.caveat: no CI/significance test on either gap; lexicality reading not excluded

caveats: Greek-fragmentation co-varies with family + vocab size + training Greek fraction (no same-family-different-tokenizer control) -> correlational, not a demonstrated mechanism
non-name-Greek falsifier RUN on Mistral but UNINFORMATIVE (cohort confounded on fragmentation AND lexicality); lexicality-matched arm owed (v2 notebook)
gemma_tokenizer_source: unsloth/gemma-2-9b (non-gated mirror, identical SentencePiece tokenizer to google/gemma-2-9b)
